name: Provide any details clarifying answer on previous question on report organization for different variant types.
value:
On the basis of my understanding I don't feel that there is still a clarity on the genomic variant analysis irrespective of constitutional and somatic variants. The world wide genomic data still shows some evident gaps, overlapping and at times also redundancy in the data, may be because of the huge population and huge diversity, and yet the prevailing down sides of NGS which is the most common type of genotyping technique being used since a decade now.

So, interpreting and releasing these large swaths of data together could be confusing and ambiguous, leading to an increase in the gaps of the data interpreted. 

Hence, publishing the  data separately can help us identify these loop holes. This will help us in a more focused and non-confused approach. It will make it clearer for us to understand the priorities while we are working with a particular genomic variant, and narrow down the gap. This will also help in the proper polygenic scoring and know the contribution of each and every genomic variant in the cancer outcome.

value:
The effect of the TKI like the Gefitinib and erlotinib drugs on the EGFR have been documented in the patients suffering NSCLC. But when there is a  minor allelic mutation in the EGFR T790M and the MET amplification , the EGFR receptors consequently grow resistant to the TKI, by causing the steric hinderances, due to a bulkier methionine side chain. This is mostly developed during the late stages of NSCLC. Most of the patients suffering with TKI resistance have shown to have MET overexpression. (1)

It has also been reported that patients with MET+ and T790M/MET + have a higher expression of c-Met, p-Met, ERBB3, and p-ERBB3. While the MAPK pathways and the AKT pathways were expressed equally in both the wild type and the mutated type, depicting higher risk of resistance to the medications.(2)

In such cases there were conflicting reports with patients being treated with Gefitinib (resistant) while when treated with Erlotinib showed efficacy, which might be interpreted as Gefitinib having its effect on the wild type EGFR while the latter having its effect on the mutated gene.(1) 

Reference:
1. T790M and acquired resistance of EGFR TKI: a literature review of clinical reports, PMID: 22263058
2. The coexistence of MET over-expression and an EGFR T790M mutation is related to acquired resistance to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors in advanced non-small cell lung cancer, PMID: 27259997

value: Interacting variants are reported in two ways.  The first is through classification of the variants at pathogenic/reportable.  This highlights their specific utility.  The second way they are presented is through the therapy in which they influence.  In the example described above you you get both variants reported in our `actionable` section and then in a therapeutic section you would see a prioritized presentation of 3rd generation TKIs with 1st generation flagged as being resistant.

value: The survey understandable focused very much on just a report for somatic NGS results.  There was a nod to reporting the results from other tests (germline NGS, HRD, etc).  This was further reflected in a single question on reporting interacting variants .  As a community we must understand that more actionable results are being gained from other tests when integrated with the somatic test. A report that integrates data from multiple tests (of course making detailed results of each available) would be more wholistic in nature and offer more treatment options.  It would be great to see future surveys/ VICC try to approach how labs comprehensively report multiple types of data and how they are usefully integrated.

value:
1.	The nomenclature of the variants is performed based on the HGVS recommendations (den Dunnen et al, 2016, Hum Mutat 37: 564-569).
2.	The classification of variants is carried out following the recommendations of the Association for Molecular Pathology, American Society of Clinical Oncology, and College of American Pathologists (AMP, Li et al. 2017, J Mol Diagn 19(1):4-23).
3.	Only variants relevant in the diagnosis, prognosis or treatment and located in the list of genes analyzed, with a total depth greater than 200X and a variant allelic frequency (VAF) greater than 0.05 are reported. Therefore, the sample may present real variants not reported because they are located in genes not included in the analysis, have no clinical relevance or are below the established sensitivity threshold.
4.	This study only use DNA from tumor sample, so germline mutations cannot be confirmed. Given the suspicion of germline variants, it is recommended that the patient be referred to a specialized predisposition to cancer unit. 
5.	The clinical interpretation of the identified variants is performed based on the current available information, therefore it may change over time as the information available increases. 
6.	This report is exclusively for qualified healthcare professionals. The decision to administer or suspend any therapy based on the mutations identified in this genetic study is the sole responsibility of the patient's doctor and must be evaluated in conjunction with the rest of the relevant information of the patient such as other diagnostic tests, physical examination and / or personal and family history.
7.	Identification of oncogenic mutations does not guarantee that associated treatment is effective regardless of tumor type. This report also makes no promise or guarantee that a drug with a potential lack of clinical benefit will in fact provide no clinical benefit.

value:
•	Intronic variants are not assessed using this method.
•	This NGS test used, does not allow definitive differentiation between germline and somatic variants 
•	Large deletions of more than 10 bp or copy number variations /chromosomal rearrangements cannot be assessed using this method. 
•	A false negative result for any variant below the LOD, i.e., 5% for SNVs and 10% for short indels, cannot be ruled out. 
•	The classification of variants of unknown significance can change over time. Please contact later for any change. 
•	Only genes included in this NGS panel have been screened. Certain genes may not be covered completely, and few mutations could be missed. Variants not detected by the assay that was performed may impact the phenotype.
•	The transcript used for clinical reporting generally represents the canonical transcript (according to Ensembl release 91 human gene model), which is usually the longest coding transcript with strong/multiple supporting evidence. Variants annotated on incomplete and nonsense mediated decay transcripts will not be reported.   
•	All precautions were taken to ensure the accuracy of these results. However, a 1% chance of error is possible in this report.

value: important points not mentioned in this survey are: 1 - How to deal withthe possibility of germline versus somatic origin of variant in the report. Some germline variants would be expected to occur in the context of constitutional syndromes that include increased incidence of cancer in addition to other features, while others are only associated with increased cancer incidence. Should both be equally reported as being possibly germline? Should the allele frequency be taken into consideration, etc. 2 - How to deal with the possibility of clonal hematopoiesis in somatic tumor analysis, etc.

value: Clinical interpretation and clinical trial information retrieval are highly unstandardized and should not be reported in an NGS report based on genomic data only. In particular, therapeutic recommendations and clinical trial availability must only be provided in a clinical interpretation workflow (MTB) with regard to all biomarkers/alterations in patient's medical record (IHC, FISH, other molecular and clinical (!) data), retrieved from curated, up-to-date clinical trial and precision oncology databases and manually curated with regard to patient's medical history. Adding clinical trial information in bulk based on a single genetic biomarker does not add any value to the report.

value:
The report must always be interpreted and considered within the clinical context, and a physician should always
consider the report along with all other pertinent information and data that a physician would prudently consider prior to
providing a diagnosis to a patient or developing and implementing a plan of care for a patient. The report should never be
considered or relied upon alone in making any diagnosis or prognosis. The manifestation of many diseases are caused by more
than one gene variant, a single gene variant may be relevant to more than one disease, and certain relevant gene variants may
not have been considered in the report. In addition, many diseases are caused or influenced by modifier genes, epigenetic factors,
environmental factors, and other variables that are not addressed by the report (or that are otherwise unknown). This report is
based on a next generation sequencing assay which does not distinguish between somatic and germline variants. If a germline
variant is in question, further testing may be recommended. As such, the relevance of the report should be interpreted in the
context of a patient's clinical manifestations.
